preamble: The shift from the alveolar nasal [n] to the velar nasal [ŋ] (ng) is a classic example of regressive place assimilation. It occurs when the mouth prepares for a following velar sound (like [k] or [g]) by moving the tongue's point of contact from the alveolar ridge back to the soft palate.

examples.note: Regressive assimilation before the velar stop [k].
Assimilation across word boundaries in connected speech.
Mandatory assimilation of /n/ before velar consonants.

sources: Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics.
Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
allophones_note: /n/ surfaces as [ŋ] in regressive assimilation before velar consonants, both within words and across word boundaries in connected speech. [n] is preserved before non-velar consonants.
